summary: Shapiro shares his discovery of Freeplans.com, a service that emails free woodworking plans supported by advertising. He reports being pleasantly surprised that the first plan he received was high-quality, with clear instructions and pictures for building a cherry bookcase. He also explains his privacy-conscious approach of using a disposable 'tearoff' email address to detect if the service sells his information.
key_points: Freeplans.com delivers genuinely useful free woodworking plans funded by ads rather than data selling
Shapiro uses disposable email addresses as a practical technique to detect spam sources and protect privacy
Early web services could earn trust through simple, transparent business models
money_quotes: Freeplans.com actually appears not to be a rip-off.
I used a 'tearoff' email address, so I'll know if they sell my name.
key_insight: Even in the early web era, Shapiro's maker curiosity and tech-savvy privacy habits were already evident in how he approached free online resources.
